title: Complex network based machine learning method for predicting circuit timing
abstract: Due to the high cost of timing signoff in the routing stage of VLSI (very large-scale integration), timing analysis in the placement stage is usually used to assist the implementation. Still, uncertainty exists due to the lack of detailed wiring information. Considering the close relationship between signal propagation and circuit topology, we propose a machine-learning method to predict the circuit timing using complex network features and construct basic and augmented models for comparison. Firstly, in the placement stage, the fundamental circuit features related to timing optimization are extracted, and the complex network features on the timing path are computed by complex network modeling, based on which the dataset for machine learning is generated. Secondly, we use machine learning algorithms to compare the performance of the two models in predicting circuit timing. Experimental results show that the augmented model combining complex network features outperforms the basic model in timing prediction accuracy. The augmented model achieves a 12.5 % reduction in MSE (mean squared error) for predicting slack, reaching 0.004515. It also shows a 1.06 % improvement in R 2 , reaching 0.9314, and a 0.54 % improvement in r, reaching 0.9652. For predicting delay, the MSE is reduced by 11.69 %, reaching 0.000302, while R 2 improves by 0.03 % to 0.9973, and r improves by 0.02 % to 0.9987. The deviation in predicting WNS (worst negative slack) is reduced by 6.53 %, reaching 5.30 %, and for TNS (total negative slack), it is reduced by 1.59 %, reaching 12.38 %. The AUC (area under the ROC (receiver operating characteristic) curve) value for identifying critical paths increases by 0.26 %, reaching 0.9873. These results demonstrate the efficiency of the proposed method in utilizing complex network features for predicting circuit timing.
section_headings: 3. Using complex network features for timing prediction
3.1. Framework of the timing prediction method
figure_captions: Fig. 1 shows the framework of the proposed method for predicting circuit timing using complex network features. It consists of two main stages: dataset construction at the top and model construction at the bottom of the figure.
Fig. 1. Framework of the proposed timing prediction method.

Fig. 3 illustrates the construction of a dataset for the timing prediction model. This process includes acquisition of the circuit and complex network features during the placement phase and timing labels during post-routing.
Fig. 2 illustrates the process of the complex network modeling. The circuit is first placed by the placer to retrieve the placement. By applying

Fig. 4 shows the prediction performance comparison of slack and delay using the metric R 2 . As can be seen, the predicted performance of the augmented model is much better than that of the basic model, which indicates the advantage of applying complex network features. Furthermore, delay prediction is better for both models than slack prediction, suggesting that the proposed method and features are more effective for delay prediction.
Fig. 4. The slack and delay comparison on R 2.

Fig. 5 illustrates a comparison of the predictive performance of WNS and TNS. As can be seen, the predictions for WNS and TNS using the augmented and basic models are almost identical and close to the real data, except for circuit D7. The TNS prediction for circuit D7 using the basic model deteriorates more than the augmented model. Circuit D7 includes fewer paths and more components, possibly leading to relative inaccuracy. Overall, the results demonstrate the efficiency of the proposed method in predicting WNS and TNS, showing a slight advantage of the augmented model.
Fig. 5. The WNS and TNS comparison.
Fig. 6. ROC curve for XGBoost path violation.
Fig. 7. ROC curve for RF path violation.
